steps.title: Step 1 — read the question and identify the model
steps.body:
A streaming service claims that $40\%$ of free-trial users go on to become paying subscribers. A manager suspects the true renewal rate is now **lower** than this. In a random sample of $20$ free-trial users, only $3$ become subscribers.

Let $X$ be the number of subscribers out of $20$ trial users. If the company's claim is correct, $X \sim B(20, 0.4)$.

steps.title: Step 2 — write the hypotheses
steps.body:
The claim being tested is $p = 0.4$, and the manager's suspicion is a **decrease**, so this is a one-tailed test:

$$
H_0: p = 0.4, \qquad H_1: p < 0.4
$$

where $p$ is the true proportion of trial users who become subscribers. Use a significance level of $5\%$.

steps.title: Step 3 — find the critical region
steps.body:
Under $H_0$, $X \sim B(20, 0.4)$. Since $H_1: p < 0.4$, the critical region is in the **lower tail**: the set of small values of $X$ so unlikely under $H_0$ that they would cast doubt on it. We need the largest $c$ such that $P(X \le c) \le 0.05$.

Cumulative probabilities: $P(X \le 3) = 0.0160$ and $P(X \le 4) = 0.0510$.

Since $0.0160 \le 0.05$ but $0.0510 > 0.05$, the critical region is $X \le 3$, with actual significance level $1.60\%$ (as close to $5\%$ as possible without exceeding it).

steps.title: Step 4 — compare the observed value to the critical region
steps.body: The observed value is $x = 3$. Since $3$ lies in the critical region $X \le 3$, the result is significant at the $5\%$ level.

steps.title: Step 5 — state the conclusion in context
steps.body: There is sufficient evidence, at the $5\%$ significance level, to reject $H_0$ and conclude that the true proportion of trial users who become subscribers is **less than $40\%$** — the manager's suspicion is supported by the data.
